meta.title: CBME Assessment Prompt Builder & RAG Agent
meta.description: Curated, source-linked database of competency-based medical education (CBME) frameworks and validated assessment tools with their reported psychometric and practical limitations.
meta.disclaimer: Guideline text and tool summaries consist of curated paraphrases of the cited primary sources. For high-stakes or accreditation use, replace these summaries with official program-specific milestone language.
meta.how_to_cite_this_tool: Zhu JHQ. CBME Assessment Prompt Builder & RAG Agent [software]. 2026. Available from: https://jhqzhu0731.github.io/medical-cbme-assessor/

rubric.name: ACGME Milestones developmental rating (Levels 1–5)

scale.label: Proficient; ready for unsupervised practice (typical graduation target)
Aspirational / expert; exceeds expectations for graduation

rubric.not_assessable: Use when the provided evidence contains no observable data for a guideline.

ACGME.label: ACGME Core Competencies (US GME)
ACGME.citation_note: Six Core Competencies codified by the ACGME Outcome Project and operationalized through the Milestones; domain summaries below paraphrase the Common Program Requirements and Milestones Guidebook.

patient_care.name: Patient Care & Procedural Skills
guidelines: Gathers essential and accurate history relevant to the clinical context
Develops prioritized differential diagnoses and management plans
Performs procedures safely with appropriate indications and consent
Adapts plans based on patient response and new data

evidence.description: Assessment of Patient Care relies on direct clinical observations and procedural audits.

tools.name: Ottawa Surgical Competency Operating Room Evaluation (O-SCORE)

evidence.design: Formative WBAs focus on measuring active procedural mechanics, history gathering, and clinical reasoning.
evidence.effectiveness: DOPS reaches G >= 0.70 reliability with 6 observations from >=3 raters. O-SCORE effectively ranks operational independence.
evidence.limitations: Subject to halo effect and leniency bias. O-SCORE is retrospective (measuring past intervention) and is insufficient alone for prospective, high-stakes entrustment. Trainees may 'cherry-pick' easy cases.
evidence.gaps: Lack of standardized rater training. Video audits (VOPS) are proposed to reduce implicit bias.

medical_knowledge.name: Medical Knowledge
guidelines: Applies evidence-based and physiologic reasoning to clinical problems
Demonstrates knowledge of diagnostic testing and therapeutics
Identifies knowledge gaps and connects them to patient care decisions

evidence.description: Medical Knowledge is measured via objective testing of factual recall and reasoning under clinical uncertainty.
evidence.design: SCT scores examinees by comparing their clinical decisions under uncertainty with the aggregated opinions of an expert panel.
evidence.effectiveness: SCT shows robust construct validity (discriminates senior vs junior cohorts).
evidence.limitations: SCT scoring can be 'gamed' by selecting central options. Highly dependent on panel size (requires >=15 experts). Multiple choice testing measures 'knows' rather than clinical application.
evidence.gaps: Long-term educational consequences of SCT remain poorly researched; missing standard-setting norms.

pbli.name: Practice-Based Learning & Improvement
guidelines: Incorporates feedback and self-assessment into improvement
Uses evidence and literature to inform decisions at the point of care
Participates in quality improvement and error analysis

evidence.description: PBLI tracks a learner's ability to self-assess, review evidence-based medicine (EBM), and perform QI.

tools.name: Fresno Test of EBM Competence / Berlin Questionnaire

evidence.design: Evaluates reflective self-assessment, QI project completion, and Point-of-Care evidence synthesis.
evidence.effectiveness: Fresno test reliably evaluates all 4 critical steps of EBM competency (ask, acquire, appraise, apply) with good inter-rater agreement.
evidence.limitations: Lack of reproducible, validated tools for the reflective subcompetency. Dashboards contain metrics outside the direct control of individual trainees. Fresno is highly resource-intensive to grade.
evidence.gaps: Missing generalizable, cross-specialty clinical registries. Need tools distinguishing EBM knowledge from actual bedside clinical practice.

communication.name: Interpersonal & Communication Skills
guidelines: Communicates clearly and empathetically with patients and families
Delivers organized, accurate handoffs and documentation
Uses shared decision-making and confirms understanding

evidence.description: Communication skills are assessed via task-based checklists and direct observation.
evidence.design: Checklist elements explicitly measure relationship-building, information-gathering, shared decision-making, and structural closure.
evidence.effectiveness: SEGUE exhibits high concurrent and construct validity; Kalamazoo Adapted (KEECC-A) is psychometrically robust and user-friendly.
evidence.limitations: Checklists risk rewarding reductionist, rote behavior ('ticking boxes') rather than authentic interaction. Inter-rater reliability of Kalamazoo remains modest. Transfer of simulated skills to real practice is poorly verified.
evidence.gaps: Few studies investigate predictive validity. Tools evaluating multiparty/family communication are severely lacking.

professionalism.name: Professionalism
guidelines: Demonstrates honesty, integrity, and accountability
Respects patient autonomy, privacy, and cultural context
Recognizes and manages conflicts of interest and personal limits

evidence.description: Professionalism relies on the observation of ethical actions, accountability, and multi-source feedback.
evidence.design: Evaluates 24 behaviorally anchored items across relationship skills, reflective skills, and time management.
evidence.effectiveness: P-MEX demonstrates strong construct validity and cross-cultural adaptability (validated in Canada, Switzerland, Japan, Finland).
evidence.limitations: High rater variation and ceiling effects (trainees score highly from the outset, limiting sensitivity). High administrative workload and time burden for observers to score and deliver feedback.
evidence.gaps: Needs longitudinal multilevel analysis. Gaps remain in capturing physician well-being and managing personal limits.

sbp.name: Systems-Based Practice
guidelines: Coordinates care across the interprofessional team and settings
Considers cost, safety, and resource stewardship
Navigates systems to advocate for patient needs

evidence.description: SBP measures system-level advocacy, cost-consciousness, and safety navigation.

tools.name: Interprofessional MSF, QI Projects, and Dashboard Review

evidence.design: Triangulates electronic record data, multisource care audits, and formal project execution.
evidence.effectiveness: Provides systemic tracking of QI execution; allows committees (CCCs) to judge system-level contribution.
evidence.limitations: The 'orphan competency' — lacks any single validated direct-observation instrument. Individual attribution of systemic patient safety outcomes is extremely difficult. Inconsistent curricula across programs.
evidence.gaps: Severe shortage of generalizable, objective SBP assessment tools. Shortage of validated methods for evaluating individual system navigation.

CanMEDS.label: CanMEDS Roles (Canada/International)
CanMEDS.citation_note: Seven CanMEDS Roles; domain summaries below paraphrase the corresponding Role chapters of the CanMEDS 2015 Physician Competency Framework.

medical_expert.name: Medical Expert
guidelines: Integrates knowledge, skills, and attitudes in clinical decision-making
Performs assessments and interventions appropriate to competence level

evidence.description: The central integrating role. Assessed using point-of-care direct observation tools and milestone metrics.
evidence.design: Applies retrospective supervision anchors (intervention needed) to measure integration of multiple intrinsic roles.
evidence.effectiveness: High construct sensitivity; OCAT/O-SCORE clearly distinguish escalating training levels.
evidence.limitations: Single-encounter observations suffer from low reliability. High rater variability; G-coefficients hover around 0.65 even with trained raters. Restrospective supervision does not directly equal prospective, high-stakes entrustment.
evidence.gaps: Requires programmatic synthesis. Gaps exist in establishing standardized, multi-institutional entrustment benchmarks.

communicator.name: Communicator
guidelines: Establishes rapport and gathers/synthesizes patient information
Shares information and plans clearly with patients and teams

evidence.description: See also ACGME 'Interpersonal & Communication Skills'. CanMEDS emphasizes patient-centered information exchange.
evidence.design: Observes critical relational skills, diagnostic accuracy, and explicit verification of patient understanding.
evidence.effectiveness: Provides structured, reproducible formats with high content validity and learner acceptability.
evidence.limitations: Consensus was not reached on consistent, cross-phase workplace assessment feasibility in Delphi studies (2 of 5 competencies unresolvable). Checklists can induce artificial, tick-box behavior.
evidence.gaps: Needs workplace-based, longitudinal predictive validity evidence.

collaborator.name: Collaborator
guidelines: Works effectively within interprofessional teams
Manages handovers and shared care safely

evidence.description: Collaborator focuses on interprofessional teamwork, safe transitions of care, and conflict resolution.
evidence.design: 31-item rubric assessing interprofessional communication, role clarity, collaborative planning, and conflict management.
evidence.effectiveness: Demonstrates excellent discriminative validity across performance levels (p < .001); internal consistency alpha is extremely high (.88 - .96).
evidence.limitations: High rates of missing data on conflict resolution items (difficult to observe when clinical teams function smoothly). Highly dependent on multi-source coordinator compliance and prolonged observation.
evidence.gaps: Validated simulations for testing conflict management are needed. Generalizability to non-academic clinics remains unproven.

leader.name: Leader
guidelines: Contributes to system improvement and resource stewardship
Manages time, priorities, and team tasks

evidence.description: The Leader role examines resource stewardship, personal leadership, and priority management.
evidence.design: 360-degree tool targeting observable self-management and team interaction behaviors.
evidence.effectiveness: Captures baseline administrative and basic team presence in junior cohorts.
evidence.limitations: LEADER tool explicitly does not evaluate larger leadership constructs (systems complexity, budgeting, leading change). Delphi workgroups report zero key Leader competencies can be consistently assessed across phases.
evidence.gaps: No validated workplace tools exist for senior-level leadership and healthcare financing.

health_advocate.name: Health Advocate
guidelines: Responds to individual patient and community health needs
Addresses determinants of health in care plans

evidence.description: Health Advocate addresses clinical-level and community-level social determinants of health. (A major gap domain.)
evidence.design: No widely adopted, validated direct-observation or performance-based instrument exists.
evidence.effectiveness: N/A. Advocacy does not systematically influence high-stakes residency outcomes.
evidence.limitations: Ambiguous definitions. Faculty report feeling unequipped or unwilling to evaluate advocacy actions. Delphi panels rate this role as not feasible for clinical workplace assessment.
evidence.gaps: Severe. Requires a shared educational vision, ethically-aware rubrics, and authentic clinical/community placement assessments.

scholar.name: Scholar
guidelines: Applies best evidence and lifelong learning to practice
Teaches and gives constructive feedback

evidence.description: Scholar encompasses teaching, scientific research appraisal, and lifelong learning.

tools.name: Fresno Test of EBM Competence / Berlin Questionnaire / ACE Tool

evidence.design: Scenario-based open questions with detailed grading rubrics (Fresno) or multiple choice questions (Berlin/ACE).
evidence.effectiveness: Fresno test shows robust construct validity and good inter-rater reliability for EBM steps.
evidence.limitations: Fresno is resource-intensive to grade (~60 mins to complete + expert grading). Berlin/ACE focus almost exclusively on critical appraisal (Step 3) rather than application. Teaching and scholarship subcompetencies lack validated instruments.
evidence.gaps: Shortage of validated bedside EBM tools. Absence of standardized, workplace-validated peer teaching evaluation metrics.

professional.name: Professional
guidelines: Demonstrates ethical practice and accountability
Maintains standards and personal well-being

evidence.description: See ACGME 'Professionalism' for core details. Focuses on societal contracts, ethics, and well-being.
evidence.design: Behavioral ratings on relationship building, boundaries, and personal accountability.
evidence.effectiveness: P-MEX shows strong structural stability and cross-cultural validity.
evidence.limitations: Ceiling effects. Assessing physician wellness and self-care remains largely unoperationalized in current scales.
evidence.gaps: Validated, non-punitive methods for evaluating and supporting personal well-being are missing.

input_types.label: transcript
input_types.method: Written record of a clinical encounter (e.g., a documented patient interaction), assessed as workplace-based evidence analogous to direct observation / mini-CEX.

input_types.label: test
input_types.method: Knowledge or written assessment; a structured measure mapped to competencies per the ACGME Assessment Guidebook.

input_types.label: video
input_types.method: Recorded direct observation of performance, assessed with a workplace-based observation instrument (e.g., mini-CEX).

input_types.label: other
input_types.method: User-defined evidence source (e.g., OSCE checklist, multisource feedback, direct observation notes).
